program: Travel English -- Europe Without a Translator
hub_subtitle: Travel English for the airport, the check-in counter, the hotel and passport control — so that Europe needs no translator

lesson.menu_title: I Have Always Loved Traveling -- The List Is Yours
lesson.menu_desc: Present perfect for the places of a lifetime: Have you ever been to...? Twenty-three cities, Positano, Capri, Paris, London -- the list has been hers for forty years, and tonight she says it out loud herself -- 39 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 12 -- I Have Always Loved Traveling
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rosângela | Lesson 12 -- I Have Always Loved Traveling
lesson.grammar_point: present perfect for life experiences (have/has + past participle, ever/never)

listenings.text: Good afternoon, everybody, and welcome. My name is Matteo, and my family has this vineyard. Before we drink anything, three minutes of my voice, and then the wine, I promise. First: where you are. You are standing on a hill, and everything you see, all the way down to that road, is grapes. My grandfather planted the first line in 1953. He never left this hill. He never went abroad, not one time in his life. He has been to Florence twice, and Florence is fifty kilometers away, and he called that a long trip. Second: what you are going to taste. Three wines, all from this countryside, all from this hill. And here is the thing I want you to understand. People ask me: Matteo, have you traveled? And I say no. I have never taken a cruise. I have never seen the coast of Ireland. I have never been on a plane. I am fifty-eight years old and I have never been on a plane, and everybody at this table has, and today you are all here, on my hill. So who has traveled? Third, and then we drink. In this room today there are people from six countries. I have shown this vineyard to people from more than forty countries in my life, and I have learned one thing from all of them. Everybody asks the same first question. Have you ever been to my country? And I always say no. And then they tell me about it. The square in their city. The castle. The island. The food their mother makes. And for three minutes I have been there. That is my traveling. Forty countries, and I have never left this hill. Now. The glasses are in front of you. Welcome.

Hello. My name is Margaret, I am sixty-nine years old, and I want to talk to you about one question. Just one. Wherever you go in this world, when you sit next to somebody, on a train, at a table, in a line at a museum, sooner or later that person is going to ask you this: have you ever been to Greece? Have you ever tried this? Have you ever seen that? It is the first question. It is always the first question. And I want to tell you what I did with it for about thirty years. I said yes. Or I said no. One word. That is all. And not because I did not have the story. I had the story! I have been to nineteen countries. I have seen the northern lights. I have eaten things I cannot pronounce. I have slept in a castle in Scotland with no heating, and I have never been so cold in my life. The stories were all in here. But somebody said, have you ever been to Norway, and I said yes. Yes. One word. And then silence. And then that person turned to somebody else, and the conversation went away from me, and I sat there with nineteen countries in my head and a cup of tea in my hand, and nobody knew. Nobody knew. That is the part that hurts, and that is why I am telling you. Now listen, because this is the whole thing, and it is small. When somebody asks you, have you ever, they are not asking for yes or no. They are opening a door. So do not answer with a word. Answer with a sentence. Yes, I have. I went in 2019, and it was the coldest week of my life. Or: no, I have never been, but I have always wanted to. That is it. That is the difference between yes, and a friend. And you do not need perfect English for it. You need the sentence. So next time, on a hill, at a table, with a stranger, do not say yes. Say what happened.

structure.title: Two words carry your whole life in them
cards: the little word -- it changes for he and she
"I have been to Italy." "She has been to Italy." I, you, we, they take HAVE. He, she, it take HAS. That is the only change there is.
the third form of the verb -- it never changes
"been", "seen", "eaten", "taken". Not the past simple: it is "I have SEEN", never "I have saw". Ten of them tonight, and you already know most.
the two keys of the question and the no
"Have you EVER been to a vineyard?" = in your whole life, one time, any time. "I have NEVER been." = zero times, in my whole life. Never already means no -- do not add a second one.

paras: It is five in the afternoon in the countryside, an hour south of Florence, and Rosângela is sitting at a long wooden table with eleven people she has never met. Behind the table there is a vineyard, and the lines of grapes go all the way down the hill and stop where the road begins. Her husband is next to her, quiet, with a glass in his hand. He does not speak English, and this afternoon he does not need to. On her other side there is a man from Ireland, and he asks her the question that travelers always ask each other -- the easiest question in the world, and the one she used to be afraid of: "So, have you ever been to Italy before?"

Rosângela puts down her glass. Twenty-three cities, she thinks. "Yes, I have," she says. "I have been to Italy three times. I have walked the coast at Positano, and I have seen Capri -- it is an island, and there are cliffs on it that go straight down into the water. I have eaten in a square in Rome at midnight. I have visited a castle in Sintra. And I have never taken a cruise -- my husband says a ship is a hotel that follows you, and we prefer the trains." The man laughs. "You have been everywhere," he says. "I have only been to Rome, and I have never seen the south. Which one is the best?" And Rosângela does something she has never done in English before: she answers a question about her own life, for two minutes, without stopping, and nobody at that table helps her.

Here is what nobody at the table knows. The list is forty years old. The landmarks, the churches, the twenty-three cities, the little train down to the coast -- all of it happened, all of it is hers, and for forty years she had every single one of those places inside her head and no way to say them abroad. In Paris, four years ago, her son said them for her. In London, at passport control, her son said them for her. He said them well -- he is fluent, and that was never the problem. The problem is that a life is not a list of places: it is the sentences you can say about them. This afternoon, on a hill in Tuscany, with a glass of red wine and a stranger from Ireland, Rosângela said her own list out loud, herself. It took her two minutes, and two words: have been.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: A man from Ireland tells Rosângela which cities in Italy she should visit next, because he knows the country better than she does.
Rosângela has been to more places than the man who is asking -- and for the first time she says her own list out loud, alone. The places were always hers. The sentences were not.
Rosângela cannot answer the question, so her husband answers it for her while she looks for the words.

items: Rosângela has been to Italy three times, and she has walked the coast at Positano.
She says it herself: "I have been to Italy three times. I have walked the coast at Positano, and I have seen Capri."
Rosângela has taken a cruise once, and she says it is worth it.
The opposite: "I have never taken a cruise." Her husband says a ship is a hotel that follows you -- they prefer the trains.
The man from Ireland has seen more of Italy than Rosângela.
He says: "I have only been to Rome, and I have never seen the south." She has been three times and has seen twenty-three cities.
For forty years Rosângela had all those places in her head, and no way to say them abroad.
That is the whole text. The list is forty years old -- and in Paris and in London her son said it for her.
Her son spoke for her in Paris because his English was not good enough to be useful.
He is fluent, and the text says that was never the problem. The problem is that a life is not a list of places: it is the sentences you can say about them.

items.situation: A stranger at a long table asks you: "Have you ever been to Italy before?" Do not answer with one word. Answer with the sentence.
tips: Yes, I have. I have been to Italy three times, and I have seen twenty-three cities.
Yes, I have -- and I have walked the coast at Positano. It is the most beautiful place I have seen.

items.situation: The same stranger asks about Capri. Tell him what it is, and what is on it. Two of tonight's words.
tips: I have been to Capri. It is an island, and the cliffs go straight down into the water.
Yes, I have. Capri is an island, and there are cliffs on the coast. It is worth it.

items.situation: He asks: "Have you ever taken a cruise?" You have not. Do not stop at no -- say the no, and then say something.
tips: No, I have never taken a cruise. My husband says a ship is a hotel that follows you.
No, I have never taken one, but I have taken almost every train in Italy.

items.situation: He asks about Ireland. You have not been -- but you want to go. Use the expression of tonight.
tips: No, I have never been to Ireland, but I have always wanted to see the cliffs.
I have never been. I have always wanted to go, and one day I will.

items.situation: Careful now -- he asks WHEN you went to Paris. It was 2022. A date is on the table.
tips: I went to Paris in 2022. (A date, so past simple -- not "I have been in 2022".)
I went in 2022, with my son. I have been to France once.

items.situation: Ask HIM the question back. He is from Ireland, and you are in Brazil in your head.
tips: Have you ever been to Brazil?
Have you ever tried Brazilian coffee? Have you ever seen Rio?

items.situation: He has one week in Italy and asks what he has to see. You have twenty-three cities behind you. Tell him.
tips: Go to the south. I have been three times, and the coast is the best part.
Go to the small towns in the countryside, not only the landmarks. And take the train.

items.situation: He says a vineyard in the countryside is a waste of a day -- the landmarks in the city are what matter. You disagree. Say why, with an experience.
tips: I disagree. I have seen the landmarks, and this hill is the thing I am going to remember.
In my opinion the countryside is worth it. I have never sat at a table like this in a city.
